content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 9, 2017

Laura E. Gasser
Assistant General Counsel
California Public Utilities Commission
Legal Division
505 Van Ness Ave.
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-16-250

Dear Ms. Gasser:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are general in nature and not limited to specific governmental decisions, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Is a senior manager with the California Public Utilities Commission (the "CPUC") prohibited from taking part in a rulemaking proceeding if a subsidiary of a company in which the manager owns more than $2,000 in stock advocates for a position in the proceeding that may affect the subsidiary's future business practices?

CONCLUSION

Provided that the manager's only interest in the company is that of a stockholder of the parent company, the subsidiary's participation in a rulemaking proceeding does not in itself

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-16-250
Page No. 2

disqualify the manager from taking part in the proceeding. While it does not generally appear that a
financial effect on the subsidiary will necessarily affect the parent company considering the relative
small amount of the parent company's revenue derived from the subsidiary in this case, each
proceeding must be analyzed on a case-by-case basis. Accordingly, we must caution that the
manager is prohibited from taking part in the decision if it is foreseeable that the decision will affect
the value of the parent company's stock and this is a determination we cannot make without
identifying the nature of a specific proceeding before the CPUC.³

FACTS

You are the Assistant General Counsel for the CPUC and seeking assistance on behalf of a
senior manager. The manager is not a Commissioner, but it is the manager's role to direct a unit of
expert staff in undertaking analysis, preparing recommendations, and participating in a wide range
of CPUC proceedings. As pertinent to this request, the manager owns Alphabet, Inc. ("Alphabet")
stock valued at more than $2,000.

Alphabet is a large corporation publicly traded on the NASDAQ. Alphabet also has a small
subsidiary, Nest Labs, Inc. ("Nest"). Alphabet is a holding company that has one large subsidiary,
Google, Inc. ("Google"), whose advertising revenues account for nearly 100% of Alphabet's
revenues. Alphabet had $75 billion in revenue for 2015 with a market capitalization that has
fluctuated between roughly $450 and $550 billion over the past year. Alphabet has a number of tiny
subsidiaries. These subsidiaries are so small in comparison to Google that Alphabet lumps them
together and calls them "Other Bets" for SEC reporting purposes. Nest is one of these Other Bets.
Together, the Other Bets generated 0.6% of Alphabet's gross revenues (or $448 million) in 2015,
whereas Google produced $74.5 billion in gross revenues.⁴

Alphabet, itself, is not directly involved in in the CPUC proceeding. Nest, however, is
involved in several large rulemaking proceedings before the CPUC. These proceedings involve
dozens, if not hundreds, of parties and issues. As makers of smart digital thermostats and other
devices for home security, you state that Nest's "interest in the proceedings appears related to
suggesting future actions that presumably may affect its future business practices."

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from taking part in governmental decisions in which
they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in

³ As you noted, the Commission repealed former Regulation 18703.1 as part of the Commission's overhaul of
its conflict of interest regulations in 2014. Under former Regulation 18703.1, an official with an interest in a parent
company also had an interest in any subsidiary and was therefore prohibited from taking part in any decision with a
reasonably foreseeable and material effect on either the parent company or the subsidiary. We note that the Commission
is currently reexamining the 2014 overhaul of its conflict of interest regulations to further refine them. It is possible that
the Commission may consider at least two issues pertinent to this advice including whether to adopt a parent-subsidiary
rule similar to former Regulation 18703.1 and changes to the materiality rule for interests in business entities publicly
traded. These amendments should be considered by the Commission within the next several months and may supersede
the analysis in this letter.

⁴ Because revenues from the Other Bets are reported to the SEC cumulatively, the official has not been able to
determine Nest's share of the 0.6% of Alphabet's revenues derived from the Other Bets.

File No. I-16-250
Page No. 3

a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's interests specified in Section 87103.

As pertinent to this request, an official has an interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) Additionally, an official has an interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more received by, or promised to, the official within the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) As the owner of more than $2,000 in Alphabet stock, the manager has an investment interest in Alphabet. Provided that the manager receives income from the stock of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision, the manager also has an interest in Alphabet as a source of income.

To analyze the potential effect of any rulemaking decisions on the manager's interests in Alphabet, the manager will need to determine whether the financial effect is both foreseeable and material. Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 1870(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 1870(b).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5.

Based upon the facts provided, Alphabet is not explicitly involved in the rulemaking proceedings. Accordingly, a financial effect must be more than hypothetical or theoretical. Moreover, a financial effect is material only "if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of a [Alphabet's] publicly traded stock . . . ." (Regulations 18702.1(b) and 18702.3(a)(4).)

However, you have only asked generally whether Nest's involvement in a rulemaking proceeding disqualifies the manager from taking part in the proceeding. Considering that Alphabet derives only 0.6% of its revenues from the Other Bets, it does not appear that a financial effect on Nest from a rulemaking proceeding will necessarily affect the value of Alphabet's stock. Accordingly, Nest's participation in a rulemaking proceeding does not in itself disqualify the manager from taking part in the proceeding. However, we caution that each proceeding must be analyzed on a case-by-case basis, and the manager is prohibited from taking part in the decision if it is foreseeable that the decision will affect the value of Alphabet's stock. If the manager has any questions regarding a specific rulemaking proceeding and whether the proceeding may affect the value of Alphabet's stock, the manager should seek further assistance identifying the nature of the proceeding. As mentioned above, Commission Staff anticipates that the Commission may revisit the parent-subsidiary rule and the materiality rule for publicly traded businesses in the near future and potential amendments may supersede this advice.

File No. 1-16-250
Page No. 4


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl
